insights.title: Standard frequency modulation scales to the parameter value, it does not offset from it
insights.summary: The standard frequency modulator chain multiplies the base Frequency parameter value by the modulator output (0-1). This means modulation sweeps from ~20 Hz up to the knob value, not around it. To modulate around a center frequency, use the Bipolar Frequency Chain instead.

insights.title: Bipolar frequency modulation does not modulate symmetrically around the set cutoff frequency
insights.summary: Despite the name, the bipolar chain adds a signed offset in normalized frequency space (0-1 mapped to 20-20000 Hz), then the standard frequency mod multiplies the result. The result is not symmetric in Hz or semitones around the base cutoff. Users expecting a classic +/- semitone vibrato from bipolar mod will be surprised.

insights.title: Use ArrayModulator (not SliderPack) for per-note filter frequency control
insights.summary: A SliderPack cannot be directly routed to control the PolyphonicFilter's frequency per note. The ArrayModulator is the correct module - it maps note number to an array of values, functioning like a SliderPack keyed to MIDI note number.

insights.title: Recommended cutoff knob setup: use FilterFreq preset and set frequency parameter to 20kHz
insights.summary: When connecting a UI knob for cutoff control via a modulator chain, set the filter's Frequency parameter to 20 kHz, place a modulator in the frequency chain, and use the FilterFreq or FilterFreq Logarithmic range preset on the modulator. The connection wizard sets knob range and suffixes automatically.

insights.title: Polyphonic Filter processes per-voice; Monophonic Filter processes the summed stereo output
insights.summary: The PolyphonicFilter runs its filter algorithm independently for each active voice (required for per-voice modulation such as velocity-to-cutoff). The MonophonicFilter processes the already-summed stereo output and is significantly cheaper for static or shared modulation. The monophonic filter is stereo-only; for multi-channel instruments requiring mono-path filtering, duplicate the filter.

insights.title: Adding a polyphonic modulator to any chain triggers per-voice processing and raises CPU cost
insights.summary: The filter automatically switches between a cheap mono path (summed buffer, 64-sample sub-blocks) and an expensive poly path (per-voice) based on whether any modulator chain contains a polyphonic modulator. Adding even a single polyphonic modulator (e.g., velocity) to any chain forces all voices to be processed independently.

insights.title: Gain modulator behavior is non-obvious: at mod value 1.0 it produces 0 dB change, at 0.0 it applies full dB attenuation
insights.summary: The gain modulation formula is dB2gain(gain * (gainMod - 1.0)). At gainMod=1.0 the result is 0 dB (unity). At gainMod=0.0 it applies the full Gain parameter value as dB attenuation. This means a gain modulator going from 0 to 1 applies maximum cut at its minimum and no cut at its maximum - the inverse of what many users expect.
insights.verify_hint: grep dB2gain in MultiChannelFilters.cpp; check RenderData::applyModValue gain branch

insights.title: Use pma node in scriptnode for filter cutoff with envelope modulation
insights.summary: In scriptnode, the recommended pattern for a cutoff knob plus envelope modulation is: connect the cutoff control to math.add (offset), connect the envelope to math.mul (scale), feed the result into the filter's freq parameter via a peak node. The pma node consolidates this into a single node.

insights.title: Historical bug: filter held sampler voices indefinitely due to hasTail() returning true
insights.summary: An older HISE version had PolyphonicFilter::hasTail() returning true, which caused the filter to prevent sampler voices from being released. The fix was to return false. This was corrected in the codebase; users on very old HISE builds should update.
insights.verify_hint: grep hasTail in hi_modules/effects/fx/Filters.h; confirm it returns false
